# fa2 — ForceAtlas2 for Python

> The fastest Python implementation of the ForceAtlas2 graph layout algorithm. Cython-optimized (10-100x speedup). Supports NetworkX, igraph, numpy, scipy sparse. 2D/3D/N-dimensional layouts. Built-in visualization, quality metrics, CLI, and MCP server for AI agents.

## Install

```
pip install fa2                  # core (Cython compiled if possible)
pip install fa2[viz]             # + matplotlib visualization
pip install fa2[mcp]             # + MCP server for AI agents
pip install fa2[viz,mcp]         # both
pip install fa2[networkx]        # + NetworkX support
pip install fa2[igraph]          # + igraph support
```

## Simple API (no numpy required)

```python
from fa2.easy import layout, visualize

# Edge list → positions dict
positions = layout([("A", "B"), ("B", "C"), ("A", "C")], mode="community", seed=42)
# Returns: {"A": (x, y), "B": (x, y), "C": (x, y)}

# Weighted edges
positions = layout([("A", "B", 5.0), ("B", "C", 1.0)], seed=42)

# Dict edges (JSON-friendly)
positions = layout([{"source": "A", "target": "B", "weight": 5.0}], seed=42)

# Adjacency dict
positions = layout({"A": ["B", "C"], "B": ["A"]}, seed=42)

# 3D layout
positions = layout([("A", "B"), ("B", "C")], dim=3, seed=42)
# Returns: {"A": (x, y, z), ...}

# One-call visualization
visualize([("A", "B"), ("B", "C")], output="png", path="graph.png")
visualize(edges, output="json", path="graph.json")  # D3.js/Sigma.js compatible
visualize(edges, output="matplotlib")                # returns matplotlib Figure
```

### Mode presets for layout() and visualize()
- "default" — standard ForceAtlas2
- "community" — LinLog + dissuade hubs, tight community clusters
- "hub-dissuade" — push high-degree nodes to periphery
- "compact" — higher gravity, tighter layout

## Full API

```python
from fa2 import ForceAtlas2

fa2 = ForceAtlas2(
    # Behavior
    linLogMode=False,                       # True for community detection
    outboundAttractionDistribution=False,   # True to push hubs to borders
    adjustSizes=False,                      # True to prevent node overlap
    edgeWeightInfluence=1.0,                # 0=ignore weights, 1=normal
    normalizeEdgeWeights=False,             # min-max normalize to [0,1]
    invertedEdgeWeightsMode=False,          # w = 1/w
    # Performance
    barnesHutOptimize=True,                 # O(n log n) approximation
    barnesHutTheta=1.2,                     # accuracy vs speed
    jitterTolerance=1.0,                    # convergence speed
    backend="auto",                         # "auto", "cython", "vectorized", "loop"
    # Tuning
    scalingRatio=2.0,                       # repulsion strength (higher = spread)
    gravity=1.0,                            # center attraction
    strongGravityMode=False,                # distance-independent gravity
    # Layout
    dim=2,                                  # 2, 3, or higher
    seed=None,                              # reproducibility
    verbose=True,                           # progress bar
)

# NetworkX graph
positions = fa2.forceatlas2_networkx_layout(G, iterations=100, weight_attr="weight",
    size_attr="size", store_pos_as="fa2_pos")

# igraph graph
layout = fa2.forceatlas2_igraph_layout(G, iterations=100, weight_attr="weight",
    size_attr="size", store_pos_as="fa2_pos")

# Raw adjacency matrix (numpy ndarray or scipy sparse)
positions = fa2.forceatlas2(adj_matrix, iterations=100, sizes=sizes_array)

# Auto-tune parameters based on graph characteristics
fa2 = ForceAtlas2.inferSettings(G, verbose=False, linLogMode=True)
```

## Visualization (requires fa2[viz])

```python
from fa2.viz import plot_layout, export_layout

# Matplotlib figure (2D or 3D)
fig = plot_layout(G, positions, color_by_degree=True, title="My Graph")

# Export formats
export_layout(G, positions, fmt="json", path="graph.json")   # D3.js/Sigma.js
export_layout(G, positions, fmt="png", path="graph.png")
export_layout(G, positions, fmt="svg", path="graph.svg")
export_layout(G, positions, fmt="gexf", path="graph.gexf")   # Gephi
export_layout(G, positions, fmt="graphml", path="graph.graphml")
```

## Quality Metrics

```python
from fa2.metrics import stress, edge_crossing_count, neighborhood_preservation

stress(G, positions)                           # lower = better (hop-count distance)
edge_crossing_count(G, positions)              # 2D only, lower = cleaner
neighborhood_preservation(G, positions, k=10)  # 0-1, higher = better
```

## CLI

```bash
python -m fa2 layout edges.json --mode community -o layout.json
python -m fa2 layout edges.csv --dim 3 --seed 42 -o layout.json
python -m fa2 render edges.json -o graph.png --title "My Graph"
python -m fa2 render edges.csv -o graph.svg
python -m fa2 metrics edges.json
echo '[["A","B"],["B","C"]]' | python -m fa2 layout -  # stdin support
```

Input formats: JSON edge list, JSON adjacency dict, CSV (source,target,weight).

## MCP Server (AI agents)

```json
{"mcpServers": {"fa2": {"command": "python", "args": ["-m", "fa2.mcp_server"]}}}
```

### Tools

**layout_graph** — Compute 2D/3D positions
- edges: [["A","B"], ["B","C",5.0]]
- iterations: int (default 100)
- dim: int (default 2)
- mode: "default" | "community" | "hub-dissuade" | "compact"
- seed: int | null
- Returns: {"node_id": [x, y], ...}

**layout_and_render** — Layout + render as base64 PNG
- edges, iterations, mode, seed, title
- Returns: base64 PNG string

**evaluate_layout** — Quality metrics
- edges, positions
- Returns: {stress: float, edge_crossings: int (2D only), neighborhood_preservation: float}

## Links

- Docs: https://bhargavchippada.github.io/forceatlas2/
- PyPI: https://pypi.org/project/fa2/
- GitHub: https://github.com/bhargavchippada/forceatlas2
- Paper: Jacomy et al. 2014, PLoS ONE 9(6): e98679
